coredata.dc:title: Bimetallic ions-doped carbon dots nanotheranostics for imaging-guided macrophage polarization/ROS scavenging in acute pancreatitis
coredata.dc:creator: Jing, Fangyuan
Zhu, Yuda
Li, Fan
Wang, Yilan
Yu, Xiuxian
Zhang, Kun
Xin, Guang
Huang, Wen
coredata.dc:description: Acute pancreatitis (AP) is a life-threatening inflammatory disease of the pancreas. When AP occurs, macrophages are recruited and stimulated to pro-inflammatory M1 phenotype, releasing pro-inflammatory factors and reactive oxygen species (ROS). Therefore, macrophage polarization is one of the most promising therapeutic strategies for AP therapy. In this study, we designed a chitosan-based inflammation-responsive nanosystem, which successfully integrates ROS scavenging, macrophage polarization and multimodal imaging for AP treatment. Through a convenient hydrothermal method, Ce and Gd bimetallic ions doped CDs (Ce/Gd-CDs) were successfully constructed, which showed excellent ROS scavenging and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) function. Then, chitosan was employed as an inflammation-responsive carrier for loading with both Ce/Gd-CDs and resveratrol (RES), forming multifunctional therapeutic agent CDs/RES@CS NPs (CRCS). The pH-sensitive chitosan carriers were able to reduce the biotoxicity of Ce/Gd-CDs and RES and prolong their in vivo circulation time, thus advancing their utilization. Taken together, the in vitro and in vivo studies of CRCS displayed a favorable synergistic potency of ROS scavenging, macrophage polarization and multimodal imaging, significantly reducing AP. Our design expands the biological application of multifunctional nano-enzymes while providing a novel strategy toward constructing microenvironment-responsive therapeutic platforms for inflammatory disease treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Acute pancreatitis
Carbon dots
Resveratrol
Chitosan
Macrophage polarization

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cytotoxicity of Ce/Gd-CDs, RES and CRCS
MRI imaging in vivo and in vitro
Establishment of AP model in vivo
Effect of CRCS on macrophage polarization
Biological and histological responses after CRCS treatment in AP mice

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation and characterization of Ce/Gd-CDs. (A) Schematic diagram of the synthesis of Ce/Gd-CDs by hydrothermal method. (B) TEM images of CDs and Ce/Gd-CDs (scale bar = 100 nm). (C-D) AFM images and thickness distribution of Ce/Gd-CDs. (E) Ele mental distribution mapping of Ce and Gd in Ce/Gd-CDs (scale bar = 50 nm). (F) Diameter distribution of Ce/Gd-CDs. (G) Photographic images of Ce/Gd-CDs under daylight and 360 nm UV illumination. (H) 3D fluorescence map of Ce/Gd-CDs. (I) An XRD diagram of Ce/Gd-CDs. (J) The FTIR spectra of Ce/Gd-CDs. (K) The XPS investigation spectra of Ce/Gd-CDs. (L) The C1s XPS spectra of Ce/Gd-CDs. (M) The Ce3d XPS spectra of Ce/Gd-CDs.

Preparation and characterization of CRCS. (A) Schematic diagram of blank CSNPs synthesized. (B-D) The particle size and zeta potential corresponding to different concentrations of CS /ratios of TPP to CS/pH. (E) Schematic representation of the synthesis of CRCS. (F-G) SEM images of CRCS (scale bar = 2 μm, 200 nm). (H) Analysis of the particle size of CRCS by DLS over 7 days. (I) Particle size and potential of CRCS. (J) UV absorption spectra of Ce/Gd-CDs, RES and CRCS. (K) In vitro release curves of CRCS at pH 5 and pH 7 conditions.

Scavenging of free radicals by CRCS. (A) Schematic representation of CRCS releasing Ce/Gd-CDs and RES in vivo to perform free radical scavenging and macrophage regulatory functions. (B) Reaction of Ce/Gd-CDs with hydrogen peroxide to produce oxygen for naked eye view and dissolved oxygen detection. (C) Changes in TMB UV absorption spectra of Ce/Gd-CDs co-incubated with Fenton’s reagent for 5 min. (D) Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra of DMPO/·OH adducts were collected after incubation with different samples for 5 min. (E) Fluorescence images of RAW264.7 cells incubated with different concentrations of CRCS (40 and 60 μg/mL) for 24 h under H2O2-induced oxidative stress. Living cells are shown in green and dead cells in red, (scale bar = 100 μm). (F) Fluorescence images of 60 μg/ml CRCS incubated with RAW264.7 for 24 h under H2O2 -induced oxidative stress. The darker the green color, the higher the ROS content, (scale bar = 50 μm). (G-H) SOD activity and MDA content in RAW264.7 cell homogenates were measured by SOD assay kit and MDA assay kit. **P < 0.01, ****P < 0.0001 vs Control group; ##P < 0.01, ####P < 0.0001 vs STC group. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

CRCS regulates macrophage polarization in vitro. M1 polarization of RAW 264.7 cells were induced with LPS. (A)Immunofluorescence assay to detect CD86, CD206 and iNOS in the cells (scale bar = 50 μm). Intensity of fluorescence was quantified by Image J analysis, n = 6. (B-D) Representative pictures of CD86, CD206 and iNOS protein bands as well as quantitative plots of protein levels, n = 3. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ****P < 0.0001 vs Control group; #P < 0.05, ##P < 0.01 vs STC group; △P < 0.05, △△P < 0.01, △△△P < 0.001 vs RES group.

CRCS has good fluorescence imaging function and MRI imaging function. (A) Confocal microscopy images of RAW264.7 cells treated with CRCS (60 μg/mL) at different time periods, (scale bar = 50 μm). (B) phantom photographs of Ce/Gd-CDs for various Gd3+ concentration. (C) The changes of relaxation rate with Gd3+ concentration. (D) MRI of AP mice before and after intravenous injection of Ce/Gd-CDs.

CRCS reduces inflammation in STC-AP mice. (A) (B) Diagram of AP model construction. Samples were collected 24 h post-modeling. (C) Representative pseudo-color images of local perfusion of the pancreas. (D) Syringomyelia of the collected sample pancreas. (E) Survival rate of mice as of the time of sample collection, n = 12. (F) Ratio of pancreas to body weight of mice at the time of sample collection, n = 6. (G) Representative images of pancreatic H&E, scale bars = 50 μm. (H) Double-blind method was used to assess pancreatic edema, inflammatory infiltration and necrosis, n = 3. **P < 0.01, ****P < 0.0001 vs Control group; #P < 0.05, ##P < 0.01 vs STC group; △P < 0.05, △△P < 0.01 vs RES group.

CRCS clears ROS and regulates macrophage M2 polarization in STC-AP mice. (A)Representative images of IL-1β and P65 protein bands. (B)(C) Quantitative plots of protein levels of IL-1β and P65, respectively, n = 3. (D)(E) Assessment of serum amylase and lipase levels by Luminex multiplex assay, n = 4. (F) DHE stained images of pancreatic tissue, (scale bar = 50 μm). (G) Fluorescence quantification of DHE staining by Image J, n = 3. (H)(I) SOD activity and MDA content in pancreatic tissue homogenates detected by the SOD kit and MDA kit, n = 3. (J)(L) Representative pictures of protein bands and quantitative protein levels of CD86, n = 3. (L)(M) Representative pictures of protein bands and quantitative protein levels of CD206, n = 3. **P < 0.01, ****P < 0.0001 vs Control group; #P < 0.05, ##P < 0.01, ###P < 0.001, ####P < 0.0001 vs STC group; △P < 0.05, △△P < 0.01 vs RES group.
Schematic diagram of CRCS preparation and its application for the treatment of AP.

head.ce:title: Bimetallic ions-doped carbon dots nanotheranostics for imaging-guided macrophage polarization/ROS scavenging in acute pancreatitis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: West China Center of Excellence for Pancreatitis, Institute of Integrated Traditional Chinese and Western Medicine, Laboratory of Ethnopharmacology, Tissue-Orientated Property of Chinese Medicine Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, West China School of Medicine, West China Hospital, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610041, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: West China Center of Excellence for Pancreatitis
Institute of Integrated Traditional Chinese and Western Medicine
Tissue-Orientated Property of Chinese Medicine Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: West China Center of Excellence for Pancreatitis, Institute of Integrated Traditional Chinese and Western Medicine, Laboratory of Ethnopharmacology, Tissue-Orientated Property of Chinese Medicine Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, West China School of Medicine, West China Hospital, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610041, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Acute pancreatitis (AP) is a life-threatening inflammatory disease of the pancreas. When AP occurs, macrophages are recruited and stimulated to pro-inflammatory M1 phenotype, releasing pro-inflammatory factors and reactive oxygen species (ROS). Therefore, macrophage polarization is one of the most promising therapeutic strategies for AP therapy. In this study, we designed a chitosan-based inflammation-responsive nanosystem, which successfully integrates ROS scavenging, macrophage polarization and multimodal imaging for AP treatment. Through a convenient hydrothermal method, Ce and Gd bimetallic ions doped CDs (Ce/Gd-CDs) were successfully constructed, which showed excellent ROS scavenging and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) function. Then, chitosan was employed as an inflammation-responsive carrier for loading with both Ce/Gd-CDs and resveratrol (RES), forming multifunctional therapeutic agent CDs/RES@CS NPs (CRCS). The pH-sensitive chitosan carriers were able to reduce the biotoxicity of Ce/Gd-CDs and RES and prolong their in vivo circulation time, thus advancing their utilization. Taken together, the in vitro and in vivo studies of CRCS displayed a favorable synergistic potency of ROS scavenging, macrophage polarization and multimodal imaging, significantly reducing AP. Our design expands the biological application of multifunctional nano-enzymes while providing a novel strategy toward constructing microenvironment-responsive therapeutic platforms for inflammatory disease treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Acute pancreatitis (AP) is a serious inflammatory disease with edema, bleeding and necrosis in the pancreatic tissue [1,2]. High macrophages infiltration in the pancreas is an early and critical event in the initial development of AP [3]. The hallmark feature of macrophages is functional polarization, and generally they can broadly undergo either classically M1 activation or alternative M2 activation [4]. The two resultant polarized phenotypes, with completely different functions. M1 macrophages can produce many classic pro-inflammatory cytokines, such as interleukin-1beta (IL-1β) and nuclear factor-kB (NF-κB) to exacerbate disease, while M2 macrophages excrete anti-inflammatory cytokines [5,6]. However, at the onset of AP, the pancreatic tissue acts as an antigen or inflammatory stimulus to cause differentiation of macrophages to pro-inflammatory type M1, releasing large amounts of pro-inflammatory factors and reactive oxygen species (ROS), thus triggering a waterfall-like cascade of inflammatory reactions [7,8]. Moreover, accumulated ROS may disrupt the oxidation–reduction balance of the microenvironment, serving as a malicious propeller in the AP progression. On the one hand, ROS can directly cause DNA damage to cells or oxidize cellular proteins and lipid components. On the other hand, excessive ROS may perform a regulatory role in stimulating macrophage differentiation toward unpopular M1 [9,10]. Therefore, regulating macrophage polarization while scavenging excessive ROS may be a potential approach for AP. However, there are formidable challenges for constructing such therapeutic systems with excellent pancreatic tissue-specific targeting and synergistic treatment.

Recently, artificial nanomaterials with multiple enzyme-like activities are widely used in microenvironmental regulation of various illnesses [11]. Compared to conventional biological enzyme molecules, nano-enzymes offer low cost, controlled synthesis, tunable catalytic activity and high stability [12]. Among them, carbon dots (CDs) are of interest due to their simple preparation, multiple enzyme-mimetic activities and fluorescent properties. Utilizing the fluorescence properties of the CDs themselves, visual tracking of the nanoparticles can be easily achieved [13]. Moreover, by maneuverable metal ions doping, it is possible to endow CDs with multiple enzymatic activities for ROS scavenging. Reports indicated that cerium-doped CDs (Ce-CDs) could exhibit both catalase (CAT) and superoxide dismutase (SOD) catalytic activities for scavenging ROS [14,15]. CDs with enzymatic activities and ultra-small nanometer size are of favorable biocompatibility and easy constructed [16]. However, it presents challenging for targeted delivery of CDs to the lesions due to their shorter circulation time and rapid metabolism by kidney. Therefore, the construction of a pancreatic inflammation-specific targeted delivery system is necessary to prolong the circulation time of CDs and improve their bioavailability.

During the onset of AP, the accumulation of inflammatory cells and excessive ROS constitute a unique inflammatory microenvironment [17,18]. The pH of AP microenvironment is lower than normal tissue due to the release of a great deal of lactic acid from immune cells [19]. Meanwhile, there exist a similar enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect in the lesion, where nanoparticles within certain range of size could accumulate in the inflammatory site through extravasation across leaky vasculature and subsequent inflammatory cell-mediated sequestration, so-called ELVIS effect [20,21]. Thence, nanocarriers with the controllable size and pH-responsive release show prospect for AP treatment. Chitosan, a natural polysaccharide with excellent biocompatibility and biodegradability, can be assembled to nanocarriers with various sizes by an ionic cross-linking method [22,23]. Furthermore, chitosan nanocarriers exhibit outstanding properties of pH-responsive release, which can effectively prolong the circulation time and accurate targeted release in acidic microenvironments such as tumors and inflammation [24,25]. These findings show the great potential of chitosan nanocarriers in treating AP.

Herein, a chitosan-based pH-responsive delivery system was constructed for pancreatic inflammatory targeted release of both bimetallic ions doped CDs (Ce/Gd-CDs) and resveratrol (RES). RES is a plant-derived polyphenolic molecule, which possesses the ability to reduce macrophage infiltration and convert classical M1 to alternative M2 [26,27]. However, the original hydrophobicity and short biological half-life of RES also enormously limit its application in clinical [28,29]. In our study, an ionic cross-linking method was performed to construct chitosan nanoparticles with both Ce/Gd-CDs and RES encapsulated, resulting in multifunctional therapeutic agent termed CDs/RES@CS NPs (CRCS). This intelligent design overcomes the shortcomings of free CDs and RES, further enhances the following features: (i) For the first time, we fabricated a chitosan-based microenvironment-responsive therapeutic platforms for efficient AP treatment. (ii) ROS scavenging, MRI, and fluorescence imaging are simultaneously granted to CDs by Ce and Gd bimetallic ion doping. (iii) Based on the versatility of CRCS, we achieved a MRI guided macrophage polarization/ROS scavenging synergistic therapy for AP (Scheme 1 ). This study will expand the biological application of multifunctional nano-enzyme and provide a novel strategy toward designing microenvironment-responsive therapeutic platforms for inflammatory disease treatment.

o-Phenylenediamine (OPD), Ce (NO3)3·6H2O, Gd (NO3)3·6H2O, resveratrol (trans-3, 4′, 5-trihydroxystilbene, 99 % purity, Res), chitosan (deacetylation ≥ 75 %, CS) and sodium tripolyphosphate (TPP) were purchased from Aladdin (China). 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), ferric sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO4·7H2O), hydrogen peroxide (30 %), ABTS, DCFH-da and DHE were purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology (Haimen, China), SOD assays reagent kit (Solarbio, China), MDA assays reagent kit (Solarbio, China), Calcein-AM (sigma, USA), propidium iodide (PI), 4,6-dia-midino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) (sigma, USA). FBS and DMEM were purchased from Gibco (Waltham, MA, USA).

The Ce/Gd-CDs were prepared by an effective hydrothermal method. Briefly, 1 mM OPD, 1 mM Ce (NO3)3·6H2O and 1 mM Gd (NO3)3·6H2O were dissolved into 50 mL of double-distilled water while being constantly stirred for 30 min to allow sufficient dissolution. A reaction vessel was filled with the combined solution, and it was heated for 8 h at 200 °C [30]. After cooling, the solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter head. The filtrate was concentrated and purified by column layer analysis. Using a vacuum freeze-dryer, a tiny portion of the filtered solution was lastly freeze-dried. The brown powder obtained was Ce/Gd-CDs, which was stored under dry conditions for further analysis.

CRCS was prepared by ionic cross-linking method. A 0.5 mg/mL solution of chitosan was prepared by dispersing low molecular weight chitosan into 1 % (v/v) acetic acid solution. 1 mg of resveratrol was dispersed in 1 mL of ethanol, and 2.5 mg of Ce/Gd-CDs were dispersed in 0.5 mL of water. Then they were added dropwise to 9 mL of chitosan solution one by one with gentle stirring (100 rpm/min). Finally, 1 mL of 1.5 mg/mL TPP aqueous solution was added dropwise to the mixed solution, and stirring was continued for 1 h. The solution was centrifuged at 8000 g for 10 min to remove impurities, and then centrifuged at 25,000 g for 30 min to obtain chitosan microspheres.

The morphology of Ce/Gd-CDs or CRCS was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Size and thickness distribution of Ce/Gd-CDs were analyzed by atomic force microscope (AFM). Size distributions of Ce/Gd-CDs and CRCS were analyzed by dynamic light scattering (DLS) and ζ-potential of CRCS was analyzed in a DLS system (Nano ZS90, Malvern). The crystal structures of Ce/Gd-CDs were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Empyrean, PANalytical). The surface chemistry of Ce/Gd-CDs were studied using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on an Escalab 250Xi (Thermo Scientific, America). The chemical structures of Ce/Gd-CDs, RES and CRCS were analyzed using a fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (PerkinElmer Spectrum spectrometer), obtained from 4000 to 400 cm−1. UV/Vis absorption spectra were tested using a UV-2450 UV/Vis spectrophotometer (Varian Cary 4000) and photoluminescence emission spectra were determined using Fluorolog 3 spectrofluorimeter (Yvon Horiba, Edison, NJ, USA) along with 3D mapping. The doping amounts of Ce, Gd ions were calculated by inductively coupled plasma emission spectrometry measurement ((iCAP 7200, Thermo Fisher)). Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra of Ce/Gd-CDs were recorded by JEOL JES-FA200. Encapsulation rate and drug loading capacity of RES and Ce/Gd-CDs were yielded by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, Agilent 1200 system HPLC, Canada).

The release of RES from CRCS at 37 °C was measured by dispersing the samples in PBS at pH 5.0 (inflammatory environment) and 7.0 (normal environment) with gentle shaking. For each measurement, 1 mL of dialysate was taken and replaced with fresh PBS solution (pH = 5.0 or 7). The amount of released RES was measured by a SpectraMax M5 microplate reader (Molecular Devices, LLC, Sunnyvale, CA, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity of Ce/Gd-CDs, RES and CRCS
ce:section.ce:para: In 96-well plates, 6 duplicates of RAW264.7 cells (1.0 × 104 cells per well) were seeded. The growth media was changed to new DMEM containing various material concentrations after the cells had been incubated at 37 °C for 24 h with 5 % CO2. The cells treated with different concentration of Ce/Gd-CDs, RES or CRCS for 24 h were washed three times with PBS. Then 10 µL of CCK-8 and 90 µL of DMEM solution were added to each well. The absorbance of each well was measured at an emission wavelength of 450 nm by SpectraMax M5 microplate reader (Molecular Devices, LLC, Sunnyvale, CA, USA).

The median lethal dose of H2O2, LD50, was selected to induce RAW264.7 cell death in vitro to further investigate the protection of CRCS against oxidative damage. RAW264.7 cells were seeded into 24-well plates at a density of 4.0 × 104 cells/well and cultured for 24 h. The cells were incubated with different concentration of CRCS for 24 h, and then H2O2 was added for co-incubation for another 2 h. After washed three times with PBS, cells were stained with calcium-AM and PI working buffer. The stained cells were observed under a fluorescence microscope (LEICA LAS X, Wetzlar, Germany). Living cells are shown in green, while dead cells are shown in red.

UV/vis absorption of the 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) reaction solution was used to test the hydroxyl radicals scavenging function of Ce/Gd-CDs. The reaction solution consisted of 5 mM TMB, 1.5 mM FeSO4, 0.2 M H2O2, and different concentrations of Ce/Gd-CDs. To quantify the photometric changes, the reaction solutions were incubated at room temperature for 5 min and then the UV/Vis absorption spectra were measured by a UV/Vis spectrophotometer.

The total antioxidant capacity of Ce/Gd-CDs was assayed according to the ABTS (2,2′-azo (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid)) total antioxidant capacity kit (Beyotime Biotechnology, China) instructions. The ABTS solution was diluted with ethanol. 0.1 mL of antioxidant solution (Ce/Gd-CDs or trolox) was added, and kept in the dark at 37 °C for 15 min. The absorbance of the remaining ABTS radical cation was measured at 734 nm using a spectrophotometer.

MDA content was evaluated using the MDA test kit. Pancreatic tissue or RAW264.7 (5 × 106) cells were gathered, combined with the extraction reagent, and centrifuged at 8,000 g for 10 min. at 4 °C. Following the addition of the MDA detection reagent, the mixture was well kept at 100 °C for 60 min, cooled, and centrifuged at 10,000 g for 10 min at room temperature. A 96-well plate containing 200 μL of supernatant was filled, and absorbance values at 532 and 600 nm were obtained.

SOD activity was measured using the SOD assay kit. The samples were centrifuged at 8,000 g for 10 min after adding the extraction reagent. SOD assay reagent was then added and properly mixed for 30 min at 37 °C. At 560 nm, the absorbance values were calculated.

ROS production in H2O2-treated RAW264.7 cells was measured under a fluorescence microscope. Fluorescence of the ROS marker 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) in living cells. Cells (5 × 104 per well) were inoculated into 24-well culture plates and incubated for 24 h. The cells were then hatched with or without 60 μg/mL CRCS for 24 h·H2O2 was then added for another 2 h. Finally, the cells were washed three times, incubated with DCFH-DA (10 μM) at 37 °C for 30 min, and washed three more times with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS). Fluorescence of the cells was observed by using a confocal microscope. ROS levels in pancreatic tissue were measured by DHE staining.

To detect the differentiation of macrophages M1 and M2 in vitro, immunofluorescence double staining was performed. Cells were inoculated into 24-well plates at a density of 4.0 × 104 and cultured for 24 h. Four groups including the control, LPS (1 μg/mL), LPS + RES(10 μM)and LPS + CRCS (60 μg/mL) were compared. The fixed cells were permeabilized with 0.2 % Triton X-100 for 10 min at room temperature. Cells were then blocked with 10 % FBS at 37 °C for 1 h and incubated with antibodies against CD86 (1:200), CD206 (1:200) and iNOS (1:200) at 4 °C overnight. After treatment with FITC-conjugated secondary antibody (1:400 dilution) for 1 h, cells were photographed under a random light reconstruction microscope (TiA1-N-STORM, Nikon, Tokyo, Japan).

To study cellular absorption, CRCS (60 μg/mL) was given to incubated RAW264.7 cells (2.0 × 104 cells/well in 24-well plate; n = 3). Cells were cultured at 37 °C and 5 % CO2 in a dark room for 0, 1, 2, 4 and 6 h. Cells were washed three times with PBS, fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde (PFA), and stained with DAPI for 10 min. Then, cells were washed 3 times. Finally, fluorescence emission of CRCS was observed and recorded with confocal laser scanning fluorescence microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MRI imaging in vivo and in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Different concentrations of CRCS were scanned with the 7 T MRI scanning system, T1-weighted signal intensity was obtained from MR images, and the reciprocal of T1 at different Gd3+ concentrations was calculated as the relaxation rate r1. AP mice were injected intravenously with CRCS (150 µL, 10 mg/mL). T1-weighted imaging was performed for small animals under the same 7 T MRI scanning system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of AP model in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: Balb/c male mice (25–30 g) were fasted 12 h prior to surgery. Mice were anesthetized intraperitoneally with 1 % sodium pentobarbital before the start of surgery. The mice were fixed to the surgical plate, the surgical site was disinfected with iodophor, and the abdominal cavity was opened along the midline of the abdomen from below the glabella. The duodenum posterior to the liver was turned over to find the connection between the duodenal papilla and the pancreaticobiliary duct. The bile duct near the hepatic hilum was clamped with micro forceps, and the 25 G needle was punctured into the duodenal intestinal wall into the pancreaticobiliary duct, and 3.5 % sodium taurocholate (STC) (100 μL/25 g) was slowly pumped at 0.1 mL/min with a microinjection pump. The needle was stopped for 3 min after the end of injection, during which attention was paid to keep the tissue moist by adding saline drops. Then the needle was released, the micro forceps were removed, the duodenum was placed back into the abdominal cavity, and the wound was sutured and disinfected. After the operation, the mice were placed on a heating plate and their status was observed at all times.

Animals in all groups were sacrificed within 24 h after induction of AP. Pancreatic tissue was harvested immediately. Blood samples were collected and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min, and 40 μL of serum were diluted to 200 μL. Serum lipase and amylase were measured by an automatic biochemical analyzer (Roche, Mannheim, Germany) according to the product specification. Some of the tissue samples were fixed with 10 % paraformaldehyde for histopathological examination, while the others were stored at −80 °C for western blot.

Continuous measurements of local blood flow in the mouse pancreas were performed by laser speckle contrast imaging (LSCI). The device was placed 25 cm perpendicular to the tissue surface and recorded blood perfusion before and after collection of the sample, with images acquired at 1 frame/5 s.

Fresh pancreatic tissue was fixed in 10 % paraformaldehyde for 24–48 h on a shaker, and then the tissue specimens were dehydrated, paraffin-embedded and sectioned. The 4-μm-thick de-paraffinized sections were stained in H&E for routine histological observation and scoring. Morphological damage to the pancreas was measured by a double-blind method based on the severity of edema, necrosis and vacuolization of pancreatic tissue according to the pathological scoring criteria of the pancreas. Assessments were performed in 10 randomized areas (magnification, ×200). Edema, inflammatory cell infiltration and vacuolar cell necrosis were graded (grade 0–3) by double-blind method. An optical microscope (ZEISS, Jena, GMBH) was used to examine each slide.

Tissue and total cellular protein samples were extracted using RIPA lysis buffer. Protein levels were identified with the bicinchoninic acid assay (BCA) (Beyotime, China). For determination of the total protein concentration, the same amount of protein from each specimen was taken for SDS-PAGE and transferred onto PVDF membranes (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA). After blocking for 1 h at ambient temperature, PVDF membranes were incubated with antimatter overnight at 4 °C: IL-1β (Abmart, China) (1:1000), p65(Abmart, China) (1:1000), iNOS (Huabio, China) (1:500), CD206 (Proteintech, USA) (1:1000), CD86 (Abclonal, China) (1:1000), GAPDH (Proteintech, USA) (1:10000). The membranes were washed 3 times and then incubated with a second antibody for 1 h at room temperature. Finally, the bands were studied by Image Lab program (Bio-Rad Laboratories, America).

Blood samples from rats were collected by cardiac puncture, centrifuged erythrocytes were aspirated, and saline was added in the appropriate ratio (1:49) to make a final concentration of 2 % of the erythrocyte suspension [31]. The erythrocyte suspension was then combined with CRCS, which had been diluted to gradient concentrations of 100, 200, and 300 μg/mL, and incubated at 37 °C for 1 h. The supernatants of samples were centrifuged, and the rate of hemolysis was measured. Using a spectrophotometer, absorbance was calculated at 415 nm. With pure water serving as a positive control and saline as a negative control, the hemolysis rate was represented as a percentage of the pure water group.

The statistical analysis was performed with the use of GraphPad Prism 8.0. Biological replicates are indicated by complete data points and values. All results were presented as the mean SD of repeated experiments, and one-way analysis of variance was used to compare group differences (ANOVA). The statistical significance of a value was defined P < 0.05.

In this work, CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method using OPD as the carbon source. Similarly, Ce (NO3)3 and Gd (NO3)3 proportionally were added to the above solution, such that bimetallic ion doped Ce/Gd-CDs were obtained (Fig. 1 A). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images indicated that the fabricated Ce/Gd-CDs exhibited as spherical nanoparticles with an average size of 16.2 nm, which is slightly larger than that of CDs (10 nm) (Fig. 1B). A more detailed morphology of Ce/Gd-CDs was detected by atomic force microscopy (AFM). The Ce/Gd-CDs were dispersed uniformly with the thickness of 0–5 nm (Fig. 1C and D). The diameter of Ce/Gd-CDs was around 15 nm (Fig. 1F). As indicated by the 3D fluorescence emission profile, Ce/Gd-CDs had a broad absorption peak in the range of 300 to 500 nm, and displayed a highest emission peak at about 560 nm (Fig. 1H). Under the irradiation of a UV lamp, a yellow-green fluorescence of CDs solution can be observed by naked eye (Fig. 1G).

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of Ce/Gd-CDs displayed two broad and low-intensity peaks at 28.0° and 41.5°, indicating a heterogeneous structure with graphene-like properties (Fig. 1I). The surface chemical bonds of Ce/Gd-CDs were studied by FTIR. The strong absorption peaks at 3200, 1620, 1400 and 1080 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of OH/ NH, CO, CC/CN and CO groups, respectively (Fig. 1J). These results indicated that numbers of hydrophilic groups were attached on the surface of Ce/Gd-CDs, suggesting their excellent solubility and dispersibility. Elemental mapping revealed that both Ce and Gd were distributed evenly within the carbon particles (Fig. 1E). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of Ce/Gd-CDs showed five major peaks at 284.8, 401.4, 532.7, 886.0 and 1187.5 eV, corresponding to the constituent C (76.12 %), N (3.91 %), O (19.34 %), Ce (0.49 %) and Gd (0.14 %), respectively (Fig. 1K). There were CC (283.5 eV) and CN (285.5 eV) in the C 1 s spectra, further proving existence of a phenazine skeleton in Ce/Gd-CDs (Fig. 1L). More importantly, in the Ce 3d spectra, peaks at 885.0 and 903.0 eV represented Ce (III), while peaks at 882.0, 890.0 and 915.0 eV indicated the presence of Ce (IV) (Fig. 1M). The ratio of the integral peak areas of Ce3+ and Ce4+ was calculated to be approximately 6:4. The enzymatic catalytic activity of the Ce-based nano-enzymes depended on the rapid conversion between Ce3+ and Ce4+, and such autocatalytic process was spontaneous, cyclic and more efficient than conventional biological enzyme. These findings revealed that Ce and Gd ions were successfully added to Ce/Gd-CDs through a practical hydrothermal approach, indicating that these ions had the potential to function as nano-enzymes for scavenging excessive ROS during AP treatment.

As a unique positively charged natural polysaccharide, chitosan can be easily constructed to a nanocarrier with other polyanions via electrostatic force [32]. In this study, we chosen sodium tripolyphosphate (TPP) as a polyanionic cross-linking agent to complex with chitosan and discussed the effect of various reaction conditions on prepared nanocarriers. As shown in Fig. 2 B, the particle sizes of prepared nanocarriers increased with the ratio of chitosan to TPP. Reports showed that medium-sized (20–200-nm-diameter) nanoparticles are optimal for passively targeting drugs to inflamed tissue [33,34]. Thus, a ratio of chitosan/TPP equal to 3 was chosen as the best mass ratio. The pH of the reaction system impacts the charge density of the chitosan chains, thus influencing their electrostatic interaction with TPP. When, the chitosan/ TPP value was fixed at 3, DLS detected that the particle size became larger as the solution pH increased, but the zeta potentials gradually decreased (Fig. 2C). The low charge density of the chitosan chains at high pH value resulted in more non-neutralized TPP in the core of the colloid, leading to the expansion of the core by electrostatic repulsion. The low charge density of the chitosan also contributed to a significant decrease in zeta potentials of nanocarriers. Furthermore, it is noted that the particle size increased with increasing the polyelectrolyte concentration (Fig. 2D).

Upon determining the optimal conditions of preparation for chitosan nanocarriers (CCS = 0.5 mg/mL, pH = 4 and CS/ TPP at 3), we directly added RES and Ce/Gd-CDs to prepare CDs/RES@CS (CRCS) nano system (Fig. 2E). Compared with pure chitosan nanoparticles, the particle size of CRCS became larger, which may be attributed to the encapsulation of RES and Ce/Gd-CDs into chitosan carriers. However, there was no significant change in the zeta potentials of nanocarriers, suggesting that RES and Ce/Gd-CDs were encapsulated within the core of the chitosan carrier rather than adsorbed on its surface (Fig. 2I and S1A). According to the UV absorption spectra, it can be seen that the absorption peak at 300–350 nm belongs to RES and the absorption peak at 450 nm belongs to Ce/Gd-CDs, indicating that CRCS successfully encapsulates RES and Ce/Gd-CDs (Fig. 2J). Similarly, the enhancement of spectroscopy (1157 and 2879 cm−1) in FTIR spectroscopy may be attributed to the loading of Ce/Gd-CDs and RES (Fig. S1A). The encapsulation efficiency (EE%) and drug loading (DL, %) of RES and Ce/Gd-CDs were detected by high performance liquid chromatography and ICP-OES. The EE and DL of RES were about 55 % and 16.7 %, while Ce/Gd-CDs were about 60 % and 25.8 %. SEM images showed that the CRCS were presented as spherical nanoparticles with an average size about 200 nm (Fig. 2F-G). In addition, we confirmed the morphology and size of CRCS by AFM further, the results were similar with SEM (Fig. S1D). The stability of the CRCS was tested by DLS and the Fig. 2H showed that the water-dispersed CRCS was stable as it was stored at room temperature and the particle size and dispersion were almost constant over 7 days. Based on the pH-sensitive stability of chitosan structure, we examined the drug release properties of CRCS under normal physiological conditions and inflammatory acidic environment (Fig. 2K). As expected, CRCS exhibited a rapid release in PBS at pH = 5, with a cumulative release of 50 % in 12 h, while keeping stability in normal PBS solution with negligible release behavior.

Studies have demonstrated that various nano-enzymes exhibit excellent radical scavenging efficiency basing on the valence cycling changes of Ce3+ and Ce4+. The catalase-like activity of prepared Ce/Gd-CDs was first verified by monitoring their reaction with H2O2. Upon adding Ce/Gd-CDs, a large number of bubbles appeared in H2O2 solution, also reflecting by the sharply elevation of oxygen content curve (Fig. 3 B and Fig. S2D). Hydroxyl radicals (·OH) are an important class of ROS molecules, which makes a great contribution to lipid oxidation, protein inactivation, DNA damage and significant cellular damage [35,36]. Then the scavenging ability of Ce/Gd-CDs for ·OH was examined using 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as an indicator. As shown in Fig. 3C, TMB solution rapidly turned blue once FeSO4 was added, with a characteristic UV absorption peak at about 650 nm, indicating the generation of ·OH [37]. However, with the addition of Ce/Gd-CDs, the color of the mixed solution gradually lightened and eventually became colorless and transparent again, and corresponding absorption peaks intensity decreased with increasing material concentration. Considering that the pH of the AP inflammatory microenvironment is significantly lower than that of normal tissues, we further investigated whether pH alteration would affect the material enzyme catalytic activity. The results showed that when pH = 5, the ability of Ce/Gd-CDs to fade TMB was not weakened, indicating that Ce/Gd-CDs still had good ROS removal ability in acidified microenvironment (Fig. 3C). In the presence of Ce/Gd-CDs in the mixture, the TMB absorbance peaks intensity decreased within 15 min reaction time (Fig. S2A and S2B). Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy further demonstrated the ·OH scavenging function of material, where 1:2:2:1 typical peak indicating the presence of ·OH were sharply decreased upon addition of Ce/Gd-CDs (Fig. 3D). We examined the total antioxidant activities of Ce/Gd-CDs. The intensity of the 734 nm absorption peak of ABTS·+ decreased significantly along with increasing concentration of Ce/Gd-CDs, demonstrating their excellent free radical scavenging capability (Fig. S2C).

Before testing the effect of the CRCS on resisting oxidative stress and restoring the redox balance of the cellular microenvironment, we first examined the cytotoxicity of the materials. As shown in the CCK8 test, the viability of the cells still kept as high as 94 % after co-culturing with 60 μg/mL CRCS. The amounts of RES (145 μM) contained in CRCS are already far above the safe concentrations of free RES (15 μM), indicating the protection of chitosan could effectively reduce the cytotoxicity of RES (Fig. S3A-C). Besides, to investigate the protection of CRCS, the median lethal dose (LD50) for cell survival were first determined. When the amount of H2O2 in the solution was raised, cell viability of RAW264.7 cell reduced (Fig. S3D). The LD50 of RAW264.7 cells was about 300 μM.

Then, H2O2 was used to constructed an oxidative stress damage model for evaluating the protection of CRCS. By calcein-AM /PI staining, we could observe amounts of dead cells under co-culturing with H2O2. In contrast, with the addition of CRCS, the cell death caused by H2O2 could be effectively reduced and such protection was in dependent of material concentration (Fig. 3E and S3E). These results demonstrate that CRCS could protect RAW264.7 cells against oxidative stress damage.

To verify that the cytoprotection of CRCS depends on their ROS scavenging function, a widely used ROS indicator, 2′-7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), was employed for tracking the changes of intracellular ROS (Fig. 3F). As expected, a strong green fluorescence was detected in cells treated with pure H2O2, indicating an obvious intracellular ROS accumulation caused by oxidative stress. Interestingly, almost none green fluorescence could be detected after co-incubating with CRCS, suggesting their ROS scavenging efficiency. In the oxidative stress environment, reductase (SOD and HO-1) secreted by cells themselves would reduce and accompanied by a strong lipid peroxidation process. Therefore, we further examined the changes of intracellular SOD expression level and lipid peroxidation product MDA after CRCS treatment. There was a significant recovery of SOD expression and reduction of MDA in the cells treated with CRCS, indicating the restoration of intracellular redox balance (Fig. 3G and 3H).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CRCS on macrophage polarization
ce:section.ce:para: In a previous study, we have verified that within the CRCS system, chitosan wrapping imparted RES with hydrophilicity and improved cytotoxicity. Then, we further tested whether chitosan nanocarriers could also improve the efficiency of RES for promoting subtype transformation of macrophages. A model of macrophage differentiation to the pro-inflammatory M1 phenotype was first constructed by LPS stimulation. As shown in the immunofluorescence staining (Fig. 4 A), there are obvious expression of CD86 and iNOS (markers for M1 phenotype), but only faint green fluorescence of CD206 detected (marker for M2 phenotype) in the LPS-treated cells. After treatment with RES and CRCS respectively, a significant decrease in the red fluorescence intensity of CD86 and iNOS could be observed, while the expression of CD206 was significantly increased, indicating the differentiation of macrophages from M1 to M2. It is noted that the efficiency of CRCS for reprograming macrophages was significantly higher than that of the free RES. This phenomenon may be due to the fact that the amount of RES loading in CRCS is much higher than the maximum safe concentration of free RES, and the slowly released RES could continuously regulate the polarization of macrophages. Meanwhile, the enhanced antiinflammation of CRCS could also be attributed to the synergism of Ce/Gd-CDs in scavenging ROS. Similar experimental results could also be verified by western blot (Fig. 4B-4D).

The superior imaging capabilities of CDs prompt us to further explore the application of the CRCS in bioimaging. The fluorescence imaging of CRCS was first characterized using confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). We found that the cell morphology did not change significantly with time, indicating that CRCS has good biocompatibility. In addition, CRCS exhibited a distinct excitation-dependent fluorescence which can be used to track the uptake process of live cells in real time. The images showed a clear green fluorescence after 1 h, with the strongest fluorescence signal appearing at 4 h. The main markers RAW264.7 cell membrane and cytoplasm indicate that CRCS can rapidly enter the cytoplasm and promote its free radical scavenging (Fig. 5 A).

To evaluate the potential of CRCS nanoprobes for MRI, the phantom images of these Ce/Gd-CDs solution were first tested on the 7 T MRI scanning system (Fig. 5B). The T1 relaxation times of Ce/Gd-CDs increased with Gd concentrations from 0 to 0.2 mM. Then, the linear fitting of 1/T1 against the Mn concentration was conducted, whose slope represents the r1 relaxation of Ce/Gd-CDs, which is calculated to be 17.17 mM-1 S -1 (Fig. 5C). Then the feasibility of CRCS as MR T1 contrast agents for pancreatic inflammation sites was tested. The pancreatic area of AP mice was imaged pre- and post-injection of CRCS and the obtained MR images are presented in Fig. 5D. Compared to the pre-injection MR image, a strong T1-weighted signal could be detected in the pancreatic tissue after injection for 1 h, which could still be observed after injection for 8 h. These results demonstrate the applicability of CRCS as FL/MRI dual-modal nanoprobes for the imaging pancreatic inflammation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biological and histological responses after CRCS treatment in AP mice
ce:section.ce:para: Before examining the role of CRCS in treating AP in vivo, their biosafety was first evaluated on the BALB/c mice intravenous injected materials. Tests on hemolysis in vitro have shown that CRCS has good biocompatibility and does not cause hemolysis (Fig. S4B). As same as the control group, the weights of CRCS-treated mice also grew slowly but steadily during the entire 15-days experimental period (Fig. S4A). Furthermore, no significant tissue lesion or inflammation were observed from the histopathology images of organs for all groups, indicating the biosafety of CRCS (Fig. S4C). Biodistribution of CRCS was investigated by observing the fluorescence intensity of individual organs of mice 12 and 24 h after administration. The results showed that the fluorescence was mainly concentrated in the pancreas 12 h after administration, further indicating that the pH-responsive chitosan nanotheranostic could passively target the site of inflammation. It was found that the fluorescence was diminished in the pancreas but enhanced in the liver after 24 h, suggesting that CRCS may be metabolized by the liver (Fig. S5). Subsequently, we constructed AP mouse models by retrograde injection of 3.5 % STC through the pancreaticobiliary duct. Then we administered 10 mg/kg RES or 60 mg/kg CRCS twice at 2 and 6 h after modeling, respectively (Fig. 6 A, 6B). Both RES and CRCS could effectively reduce the mortality of AP mice, while CRCS group had a higher survival rate (Fig. 6E). Studies have shown that reduced perfusion of blood circulation in pancreatic tissue is closely related to the severity of pancreatitis. Therefore, we first examined the microcirculation of pancreatic tissue before and after treatment using laser scatter flow imager. As shown in Fig. 6C, compared with the control group, the blood perfusion in the pancreatic tissue of AP mice was remarkably decreased. The blood perfusion was significantly restored after RES or CRCS treatment, and the perfusion values for CRCS group was significantly higher than that of RES group, indicating that CRCS efficiently improved pancreatic microcirculation in AP mice.

Comparison of pancreatic tissue photos also showed that the CRCS treatment obviously reduced the edema of pancreatic tissue and decreased the yellow pus-like secretion attached to the tissue surface (Fig. 6D). The reduction in pancreatic mass ratio in CRCS-treated group also indicated a reduction in edema (Fig. 6F). Next, we performed histopathological analysis of the collected pancreatic tissues (Fig. 6G). The results showed that there was obvious widening of intralobular spaces (edema), massive necrosis and inflammatory infiltration in the sections of pancreatic tissue of AP mice. In contrast, the pancreatic tissue of mice injected with RES or CRCS showed significantly reduced damage of pancreas. Score of pancreatic histopathology also showed that, compared to the free RES-treated group, the CRCS-treated group had considerably lower scores in all three scoring items: edema, necrosis, and inflammatory infiltration (Fig. 6H). The significant effect of CRCS on AP can be attributed to the prolongation of the in vivo circulation time of RES and CDs by chitosan carriers, thus increasing pancreatic enrichment, macrophage reprograming and ROS scavenging.

Serum amylase and lipase levels are important indicators for the clinical diagnosis of AP, and they almost returned to normal levels after CRCS treatment (Fig. 7 D and 7E). Meanwhile, inflammatory factors IL-1β and NF-κB (p65) were also found to be significantly decreased. (Fig. 7A-C). We performed further experiments to verify whether the anti-inflammatory effect of CRCS depends on their capabilities in restoring redox homeostasis and regulating macrophage polarization. Similar to the results of in vitro cellular experiments, there could also detected a significant reduction of MDA content and enhancement of SOD activity in pancreatic tissue after CRCS treatment (Fig. 7H and 7I). In addition, ROS staining was performed with DHE on frozen sections from pancreatic tissue. The results revealed that ROS was significantly reduced in the CRCS group compared to the STC group (Fig. 7F). CD86 and CD206 were used as markers of M1 and M2 macrophages, respectively, to assess the trend of macrophage polarization in pancreatic tissue before and after treatment (Fig. 7J-L). In the AP mice, the expression of CD86 was significantly higher than that of CD206, indicating that most macrophages differentiated towards M1 during the pathogenesis of AP. However, after RES or CRCS treatment, the expression of CD206 increased while that of CD86 decreased, suggesting a differentiation process from malicious M1 to anti-inflammatory M2. Noteworthy, under the protection and inflammation-specific targeting of chitosan carriers, CRCS exhibited significant macrophage reprograming through the synergisms of ROS scavenging and RES pharmacological effects.

In summary, CRCS was developed as the inflammation-sensitive therapeutic agent to fulfil the combination of dual-modal bioimaging, ROS scavenging and controlled macrophage regulation for in vitro and in vivo AP treatment. A convenient hydrothermal method was employed for fabricating Ce and Gd bimetallic ion doped Ce/Gd-CDs, which show excellent fluorescence properties, MRI imaging capabilities and ROS scavenging effects. Protected by the chitosan carrier, the constructed CRCS could efficiently load Ce/Gd-CDs and RES, prolong in vivo circulation time, and achieve responsive drug release at pancreatic inflammatory sites. The in vitro and in vivo results demonstrate that CRCS could effectively remove excessive ROS at pancreatic inflammatory site and promote the conversion of macrophages from M1 to M2, exert a synergism in the treatment of AP. Our results open a new avenue for constructing microenvironment-responsive theranostic platforms for inflammatory disease treatment.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: Fangyuan Jing, Yuda Zhu and Fan Li contributed to the work equally and should be regarded as co-first authors. Wen Huang and Guang Xin are both corresponding authors. The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Grant-in-aid for scientific research from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 81973580), the Key Technology Research and Development Program of Sichuan Province of China (2022YFS0425, 2022YFS0426), Innovative Chinese Medicine and Health Products Research Academician Workstation of Academician Zhang Boli and Academician Zhu Beiwei, West China Hospital, Sichuan University (Grant No. HXYS19001 and HXYS19002), the COVID-19 Science and Technology Emergency Project of Sichuan Province of China (Grant No. 2021YFS0408), 1·3·5 Project for Disciplines of Excellence, West China Hospital, Sichuan University (Grant No. ZYXY21002), and the Innovative Chinese Medicine Preclinical Research Fund of “Liqing No. 2”, West China Hospital, Sichuan University (161200012).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.142675.
